gloss: ritual chanting of words of power
definition: To *incant* is to produce a magical effect by chanting or reciting words of power — set formulas, divine names, or *voces magicae* — sung or spoken over a person, image, or object. It names the verbal engine of a rite rather than its whole procedure: where a *charm* is the binding song itself, to incant is the act of pronouncing it. The incantation does the work that gesture and material alone cannot.
history: Latin *incantare* meant 'to sing a spell upon' (*in-* + *cantare*, 'to sing') — the same word that, worn smooth through French, became 'enchant.' The turn is that English borrowed it *twice*: once early and softened into *enchant*, once late and bookish as *incant*, so incantation and enchantment are one Latin word wearing two faces. The learned form stayed technical — *incantatio* is among the commonest terms in the Renaissance magical corpus, the ritual use of chanted words to invoke spirits or channel celestial influence.

def_sources.label: Attrell & Porreca, Picatrix (Aristotle on magical incantations)

etymology.origin: Latin incantare 'to sing a spell on' — the learned doublet of enchant
etymology.note: 'Incantation' and 'enchantment' are the SAME Latin word: one borrowed early and worn smooth through French, one borrowed late and bookish.
etymology.hook: Enchant and incant: one word, borrowed twice.

flavor_snippets.html: It's not the most subtle <mark>incantation</mark>, but it gets the point across.

"Earth to æther, window to wonder, stillness to the sky."
—Glazing <mark>incantation</mark>

"Walls of a castle are made out of stone,/ Walls of a house out of bricks or of wood./ My walls are made out of magic alone,/ Stronger than any that ever have stood."
—Chrysoberyl Earthsdaughter, *<mark>Incantations</mark>*

"Into amethyst, I pour
Envy's shadow, sorrow's gleam.
Lure the one whose scorn I bore
That I may hear one final scream."
—Barrow witch <mark>incantation</mark>

"Taste the sweetness, breath for breath. Keep the balance, death for death."
—Barrow witch <mark>incantation</mark>

"Hone the knife and mourn the least. Three chimes to sound the sweet, grim feast."
—Barrow witch <mark>incantation</mark>
